questions.question: How many total descendants of Jacob entered Egypt according to the OSB tradition?

candidates.text: Hold to the number the Church received: seventy-five souls went down into Egypt, as the holy Stephen counted before the council. Where the Hebrew tradition reads seventy, follow the Septuagint and Stephen's tongue: 75 descended, that a multitude might be drawn up. The Apostles themselves read the Greek count we read.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid
candidates.sourceClaim: Acts 7:14 gives the count of seventy-five persons in Joseph's family.

candidates.text: Lock in 75: Stephen says seventy-five souls came down into Egypt.
candidates.sourceClaim: Acts 7:14 gives seventy-five as the number of Joseph's family members called into Egypt.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Stephen's count is sourced, but the claims about apostolic reading and Church reception are not.

questions.question: Which of these was NOT named among the sons of Jacob who came into Egypt in this chapter?

candidates.text: Joseph is missing from the list of those who 'came into' Egypt because he had gone down before them, into affliction and then into glory, to make ready a place for his kin. Picture the eleven arriving as strangers at the gate while the rejected brother already reigns within it — the one who descends first to gather his brethren after him.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Picture eleven brothers at the gate while Joseph is already inside Egypt. He is the one not named among those who 'came into' Egypt here.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 1:5 in Brenton's Septuagint says Joseph was already in Egypt while listing Jacob's family.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The image works, but it adds factual and typological claims without support.

questions.question: A new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.

candidates.text: A king arose 'who knew not Joseph' — and in the ancient world to 'know' is to stand in covenant relationship. This Pharaoh does not merely forget; he repudiates the whole bond Joseph made, and severing covenant is what unleashes the death that follows. When a people forgets its deliverer, it is already enslaved before the first whip falls.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: New king, no Joseph: the verse says a new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 1:8 says a new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The covenant-language interpretation is not supported by the cited source or URL.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT one of the store cities the Israelites were forced to build?

candidates.text: Store cities: 'POR' — Pithom, On, Raamses. Goshen was where Israel LIVED, given them in Joseph's day; POR is what their backs built in bondage. Do not confuse the place of rest with the work site.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: POR = Pithom, On, and Raamses. Goshen is the trap answer, not one of the store cities.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 1:11 in Brenton's Septuagint lists Pitho, Ramesses, and On as the cities the Israelites built for Pharaoh.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; POR is catchy, but the Goshen explanation is a factual claim without a source.

questions.question: Name one of the three store cities the Israelites built for Pharaoh.

candidates.text: Pour your labor into 'POR' — Pithom, On (Heliopolis), Raamses. Three storehouse cities that held Pharaoh's grain; Israel's sweat filled the granaries of the very land that enslaved them.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Pour the answer into POR: Pithom, On, Raamses.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; The POR hook is good, but the grain-and-granary detail is unsupported.

questions.question: Complete the verse: 'The more they afflicted them, the more they ___ and grew.'

candidates.text: Picture Egypt pressing down harder and harder, and Israel only swelling larger under the weight — a seed bursts only when buried. The harder the world presses, the wider the promise to Abraham spreads; affliction cannot cancel a divine oath, the press only forces the increase.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Afflicted? Multiplied. Pressed? Increased.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The opening image is strong, but the promise-and-oath explanation is unsourced.

questions.question: What were the names of the two Hebrew midwives?

candidates.text: 'Shiphrah ships them, Puah pulls them' — say it 'She-frah and Poo-ah.' Two midwives who feared God above the throne; Scripture preserves their names while letting the great king's name fall into silence.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Shiphrah ships them; Puah pulls them.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The names hook is memorable, but the unnamed-Pharaoh claim should be sourced or cut.

questions.question: The midwives claimed the Hebrew women were too lively to wait for them to arrive in time.

candidates.text: 'Lively ladies, late midwives' — the Hebrew women bore before the midwives could arrive. True. Where God means to multiply His people, even the means of death arrive too late; life outruns the executioner.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Lively ladies, late midwives: true.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The final life-outruns-death line is a theological claim without support.

questions.question: Because the midwives feared God, He provided ___ for them.

candidates.text: Because they feared God rather than the king, He 'made them households' — a measure-for-measure reward. They guarded the houses of Israel from being emptied of sons, so God filled their own houses with children. In the biblical economy, to shelter life is repaid with life and lineage.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Feared God -> households. Tie the reward to the reason.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 1:21 says that because the midwives feared God, He made households for them.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The measure-for-measure reward reading is not directly supported by the cited source.

questions.question: Into what did Pharaoh command that every newborn Hebrew son be cast?

candidates.text: Picture the river Egypt worshiped as a life-giving god turned into a drowning-tomb for infants. By water Pharaoh means to destroy, and by water God will destroy him at the Sea — the very element of his cruelty becomes the instrument of his judgment, while one child floating in a basket overturns the whole decree.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Pharaoh points at the river: every Hebrew son goes in. That one word is the answer.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 1:22 says Pharaoh commanded every Hebrew son to be cast into the river.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Nile-worship and sea-judgment claims need sources; the core river image is enough.

questions.question: How long was the infant Moses hidden by his mother?

candidates.text: 'Hidden a trimester' — three months, the length of a pregnancy's first stage. Moses lay concealed as long as a hidden pregnancy can be hidden, until he had to be 'born' a second time onto the waters of the Nile.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: 3M: Moses' mama managed three months.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; The trimester hook is workable, but the second-birth typology is extra and unsourced.

questions.question: From what materials was the ark (basket) for baby Moses made?

candidates.text: The Greek calls Moses' basket of bulrushes an 'ark' — the same word as Noah's — sealed with asphalt as Noah's was sealed with pitch. Twice God shuts His chosen into a sealed vessel and carries them living through the waters of death; the little ark on the Nile preaches the same salvation the great ark did.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: BA beats GP: Bulrushes and asphalt, not gopher wood and pitch.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The 'same Greek word as Noah's ark' claim is not verified as written.

questions.question: Why did Pharaoh's daughter go down to the river?

candidates.text: Picture the daughter of the king who decreed drowning wading into the very river her father made a grave — and lifting a crying Hebrew son OUT of it alive. The tyrant's own house becomes the rescue and the nursery of Israel's deliverer; God so orders events that the destroyer's daughter undoes him.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Bath becomes rescue: Pharaoh's daughter goes down to bathe and comes up with Moses.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 2:5 says Pharaoh's daughter went down to the river to bathe and saw the basket.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The rescue scene is vivid, but the providence-heavy explanation should be sourced or trimmed.

questions.question: Who was paid to nurse her own child, Moses?

candidates.text: 'Jo-keb-ed got a paycheck' — Jochebed, Moses' own mother, is hired and PAID from Pharaoh's household to nurse her own son. God turns the genocidal court into the wages of redemption, financing the deliverer it means to destroy.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Jo-keb-ed got a paycheck.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The paycheck hook is strong, but the extra redemption language is unsourced.

questions.question: Moses went out to his brethren and saw their ___.

candidates.text: Reared amid the pleasures of the court, Moses goes out and chooses to see his brethren's suffering rather than enjoy his own ease. This is the soul's first ascent: to lay down comfort and take up the affliction of God's people, esteeming reproach with them greater than the treasures of Egypt — prefiguring the greater Deliverer who, being rich, became poor for our sake.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Blank = suffering; Hebrews remembers Moses choosing affliction with God's people over Egypt's pleasures.
candidates.sourceClaim: Hebrews 11:25 says Moses chose to suffer affliction with the people of God rather than enjoy the passing pleasures of sin.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; The key point is good, but the aid is too long and adds unsourced typology.

questions.question: Where did Moses hide the Egyptian he had killed?

candidates.text: Picture Moses glancing left and right, frantically kicking sand over a body — yet no grave is deep enough to hide a deed from the eyes of God or the tongue of men. By the next day a Hebrew throws it in his face: what is covered in sand surfaces in speech, and the prince must flee.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Left, right, no one; strike, bury, sand.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 2:12 says Moses looked both ways, killed the Egyptian, and hid him in the sand.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The moral lesson is fine, but the core scene should stay tighter and sourced.

questions.question: To what land did Moses flee after Pharaoh sought to kill him?

candidates.text: Moses runs to MIDian to hide in the MIDdle of the wilderness, and the prince becomes a shepherd. The desert exile is the forge: the man who would lead a people through the wilderness must first be emptied in it for forty years.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Moses flees to Midian and drops beside a well.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 2:15 says Moses fled to the land of Midian and sat down by a well.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The MIDian pun works, but the forty-year forge line needs a source.

questions.question: Moses helped his future wife's family by saving them from shepherds at the well.

candidates.text: Picture Moses, fresh off the run, driving off bullying shepherds so seven sisters can water their flock. The shepherd's heart is shown before he is given the flock of Israel — and at a well, as so often in Scripture, a man meets the bride God has prepared. True.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Bullies at the well, Moses to the rescue, seven sisters saved. True.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 2:17 says shepherds drove the daughters away, but Moses stood up, helped them, and watered their flock.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The bride-at-the-well pattern is interpretive and should not ride without support.

questions.question: By which two names is Moses' father-in-law known?

candidates.text: 'JR' — Jethro and Reuel (Raguel), one priest of Midian under two names. Reuel the personal name, Jethro the title. Same father-in-law; do not let the two labels split him into two men.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: JR = Jethro and Reuel.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The two-name fact is fine, but calling Jethro a title is not verified here.

questions.question: What was the name of Moses' firstborn son?

candidates.text: Hear the 'GER' (Hebrew for stranger) in GERshom. Moses brands his firstborn with his own exile: 'I have been a sojourner in a foreign land.' The deliverer names his son for displacement before he ever leads a people home.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Gershom is the name tied to Moses saying he had been a stranger in a foreign land.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 2:22 says Moses named his son Gershom because he had been a stranger in a foreign land.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The Hebrew-word note needs support; also, acceptableAnswers includes 'gershon,' which may be an internal inconsistency.

questions.question: What does the name Gershom mean?

candidates.text: GERshom = 'a stranger there' — hear the 'GER,' the sojourner with no inheritance in the land. By his son's name Moses confesses that this world is not his resting place — a fitting name for the man who will lead a whole nation of sojourners.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Gershom means stranger there, tied to Moses saying he was a stranger in a foreign land.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 2:22 links the name Gershom to Moses saying he had been a stranger in a foreign land.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The meaning is right, but the extra pilgrim language goes beyond the source.

questions.question: At the end of chapter 2, God remembered His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

candidates.text: God 'remembering' His covenant does not mean He forgot. Picture the cries of Israel rising like smoke and the Lord rising to act: in Scripture, to remember is to move on behalf of a promise. His remembrance here is the trigger of the whole Exodus, deliverance set in motion.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Heard. Remembered. Saw. Knew. Rescue is starting.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The explanation of divine remembering needs a source even though the verse itself is correct.

questions.question: By what name is the mountain of God called where Moses saw the burning bush?

candidates.text: 'HOReb is where God said HELLO' to Moses — the mountain of God, also called Sinai. One mountain, two names: where the bush now burns, the Law will later thunder on the very same height.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: HOReb = HOLY hello. Horeb is where God calls Moses from the burning bush.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Adds the Sinai/same-height claim without auditable support.

questions.question: The bush burned with fire but was not consumed.

candidates.text: The bush that burns yet is not consumed is the great image of the Ever-Virgin, who received the divine Fire in her womb and was not destroyed but made whole. The Light shone forth from the thorns of our nature and burned them not; the radiance of God illumines without devouring. This is the icon of the Unburnt Bush.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses; Orthodox hymnography (the icon of the Unburnt Bush)
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Burning, not burnt: the Unburnt Bush is an Orthodox image of the Virgin who bore the divine Fire and was not consumed.
candidates.source: Orthodox Church in America, Unburnt Bush Icon of the Mother of God
candidates.sourceClaim: The OCA page says the Unburnt Bush is an Old Testament prototype of the Mother of God and that the bush burned without being consumed.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Strong Orthodox typology, but it has no usable URL and is too long.

questions.question: Moses removed his cloak because he was standing on holy ground.

candidates.text: Not cloak — SANDALS. 'Holy ground, shoes off.' Moses removed his sandals, not his cloak; the dead skins bound to the feet must be put off before the living God. The False here is a trap on the wrong garment.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Not cloak—sandals. Holy ground means shoes off.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Adds the 'dead skins' teaching without a source.

questions.question: What did God command Moses to remove because he stood on holy ground?

candidates.text: 'Loose the sandals from your feet, for the place is holy ground.' Gregory teaches that the sandals, the lifeless skins upon the feet, are the dead coverings of earthly attachment; the soul ascending to God must strip away whatever is mortal that clings to it. Bare and unencumbered, only so may one tread where God is.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Sandals off: Gregory reads Moses' ascent as shedding attachment before drawing near to God.
candidates.source: Orthodox Arts Journal, Divine Patterns in the Life of Moses
candidates.sourceClaim: The linked article says Moses' ascent involves removal of the sandals and purification from attachment to peripheral things.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The Gregory reading is useful, but it needs an auditable URL.

questions.question: What was Moses' first response when God called to him from the bush?

candidates.text: 'Here I am' (Hebrew hineni) is the called one's whole-self answer — the very word Abraham, Samuel, and Isaiah each give when God speaks their name. Moses says it before he hears a single command: not 'what do You want?' but 'I am present, I am Yours.'
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Hineni memory hook: Abraham, Samuel, and Isaiah also answer God's call with 'Here I am.'
candidates.sourceClaim: The linked passages show Abraham, Samuel, and Isaiah answering God's call with 'Here I am' or 'Here am I.'
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Good Bible-pattern hook, but the cross-references need URLs.

questions.question: God identified Himself to Moses as the God of which three patriarchs?

candidates.text: 'God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' — A-I-J in order, the covenant title that binds the whole story into one act. And because He calls Himself their God still, our Lord taught that these patriarchs live; for He is not God of the dead but of the living, and the three names are a pledge of resurrection.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: A-I-J: Abraham, Isaac, Jacob. Jesus uses those three names to teach that God is not God of the dead but of the living.
candidates.sourceClaim: Matthew 22:31-32 says, 'I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob' and adds that God is not the God of the dead but of the living.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Excellent resurrection hook, but the Jesus citation needs a URL.

questions.question: The Promised Land is described as a land flowing with milk and ___.

candidates.text: Picture rivers of milk and golden honey running down the very hills — abundance you can wade through, the opposite of the brickyard's dust. The land where whips fell gives way to a land that drips with rest; yet the milk and honey are but a shadow of the true homeland prepared for the people of God.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Picture hills dripping with milk and golden honey—the exact opposite of Egypt's brick dust.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Ends with unsourced heavenly-homeland typology and runs long.

questions.topic: Land Flowing with Milk and Honey
questions.question: Which of these peoples was NOT listed as dwelling in the Promised Land?

candidates.text: The seven dwellers: Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites — 'CHAP GHJ.' No Philistines on the list; they are Aegean sea-peoples who arrive on the coast later, never numbered among these seven.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: CHAP GHJ = Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites. No P—Philistines are not on the list.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Adds an unsourced history lesson about Philistines and sea-peoples.

questions.topic: Land Flowing with Milk and Honey
questions.question: Name one of the seven peoples dwelling in the land flowing with milk and honey.

candidates.text: Seven nations to dispossess: Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites — 'CHAP GHJ.' Seven is the number of fullness: the land is fully occupied, and God promises to fully give it. Name any of the seven.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Seven names, one will do: CHAP GHJ. Any one of those seven peoples is a right answer.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Adds the 'number of fullness' interpretation without a source.

questions.question: Complete Moses' question: 'Who am I that I should go to Pharaoh and bring the children of Israel out of ___?'

candidates.text: When Moses protests 'Who am I?', God answers not with Moses' qualifications but with His own presence: 'I will be with you.' The credential is never the messenger's worth but the One who sends him — the servant is nothing; the One who goes with him is everything.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Moses asks, 'Who am I?' God answers, 'I will be with you.' The answer is not Moses' résumé but God's presence.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 3:11-12 records Moses asking, 'Who am I?' and God answering, 'I will be with thee.'
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Solid contrast, but the Exodus 3 citation needs a URL.

questions.question: By what name did God reveal Himself to Moses (OSB rendering)?

candidates.text: In the OSB/Septuagint God names Himself 'I AM THE EXISTING ONE' — He alone truly IS, the source of all being, while creation exists only by sharing in Him. This is why the Church inscribes 'Ho On,' The Existing One, in the halo of Christ on every icon: the same I AM who spoke from the bush is He who said 'Before Abraham was, I AM.'
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: EXISTING ONE = the One who just IS. Snap on the opening words: I AM THE EXISTING ONE.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; Too dense for recall and stacks several claims that need auditing.

questions.question: How does the OSB render the name God told Moses to give the children of Israel?
questions.answer: The Existing One sent me to you
options: The Existing One sent me to you
The Lord of Hosts sent me

candidates.text: 'The Existing One sent me to you' is the messenger-form of the Name of Him who simply IS, who depends on nothing and from whom all dependence flows. This self-existent God of the bush is the very One who later stands in the flesh and declares, 'Before Abraham was, I AM' — the divine Name Christ claims as His own.
candidates.source: John 8:58; St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Turn the Name into a message: THE EXISTING ONE sent me to you.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; Too dense and leans on unsupplied Christological sourcing.

questions.question: How many times did Moses object or argue with the Lord before the Lord's anger was kindled?

candidates.text: 'Five fingers, five excuses' — count Moses' objections off on one hand, and the fifth ('send another') is what kindled the Lord's anger. God bears with much hesitation, but the refusal of the call itself, not the fear behind it, provokes Him.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Five fingers, five objections; finger five is 'send someone else,' and then the Lord's anger burns.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 4 records Moses asking God to appoint another able person and then says the Lord was greatly angered against Moses.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The counting hook works, but the fifth-objection claim needs a source.

questions.question: Which of these was NOT one of the three signs God gave Moses to perform?
options: Hand that becomes leprous and is healed

candidates.text: Three signs: Rod-to-serpent, Hand-to-leprous, Water-to-blood. The blossoming almond staff is AARON's later sign (Numbers 17), when God chose the priesthood by a branch that budded — do not smuggle it back into Moses' three.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Three signs: snake, snow-hand, blood-water. Almond blossoms are the odd one out.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; Good contrast, but it is longer than it needs to be.

questions.question: In the first sign, Moses' rod became a ___.

candidates.text: Moses' shepherd's staff drops and hisses to life as a serpent, and he recoils — a direct challenge to Pharaoh, whose crown bore the rearing cobra, the uraeus that guarded Egypt's throne. God seizes the very emblem of Pharaoh's power and makes it writhe in a shepherd's hand; taking it up by the tail, Moses grasps the enemy he is sent to overcome.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Pharaoh wore the rearing cobra as a royal emblem, so Moses' serpent-sign hits Egypt right on the crown.
candidates.source: BibleHub Brenton, Exodus 4; Metropolitan Museum of Art, Uraeus
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 4 says the rod became a serpent, and the Met says the cobra worn over the king's brow is called a uraeus and is a symbol of royalty.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The uraeus history hook needs an auditable source URL.

questions.question: In the second sign, Moses' hand became ___ when he drew it from his bosom.

candidates.text: Picture Moses drawing his hand from his bosom dead-white with leprosy, then tucking it back and drawing it out whole. In one gesture God shows He both wounds and heals the flesh — the same power that can strike Egypt can as swiftly cleanse, judgment and healing flowing from one bosom.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Moses pulls out a snow-white hand, hides it again, and pulls it out normal.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The image slides into unsourced teaching about God wounding and healing.

questions.question: In the third sign, water from the river poured on dry land would become ___.

candidates.text: The third sign turns river-water poured on dry ground into blood — the dress rehearsal for plague one, and the target is deliberate. The Nile was worshiped as a god, so the first blow of judgment falls on Egypt's river-deity; the sign in Moses' hand is the seed of the judgment that will redden Egypt's worshiped river.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: River to blood is the preview of plague one, and Egypt even personified the Nile in the god Hapi.
candidates.source: BibleHub Brenton, Exodus 4 and 7; Encyclopaedia Britannica, Hapi
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 4 and 7 describe river water becoming blood, and Britannica identifies Hapi as the ancient Egyptian personification of the Nile's inundation.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The current source does not support the Nile-god claim.

questions.question: Complete Moses' complaint: 'I am weak in speech and slow of ___.'

candidates.text: 'Slow of tongue, but sent.' Moses pleads a stammering mouth, yet God chooses the weak speaker to confront the mightiest king on earth — then supplies Aaron's lips, so when Pharaoh falls no one can credit Moses' eloquence. The weakness guarantees the glory stays God's.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Slow of tongue. Tongue is the word you need.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Adds unsourced explanation and overstates with guarantee language.

questions.question: Whom did God appoint to be Moses' spokesman before Pharaoh?

candidates.text: 'Aaron the mouth, Moses the message' — A for Aaron, A for Articulate. Note the order: the word originates with Moses who hears God, and Aaron speaks it — the prophet-and-spokesman pattern, the divine word handed down and proclaimed.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Aaron = the mouth. Moses gets the message; Aaron says it out loud.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Adds an unsourced prophet-and-spokesman teaching layer.

questions.question: Complete God's words to Pharaoh: 'Israel is My ___ son.'

candidates.text: 'Israel is My firstborn son' — God claims the nation as His own child and heir among the peoples, and herein lies the legal ground of the last plague: Egypt seized and tried to murder God's firstborn, so Egypt's firstborn must answer. The whole Passover turns on this status of firstborn and inheritance, the shadow of the true Son called out of Egypt.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Firstborn is not just first in line; it is heir-status. So 'Israel is My firstborn son' marks Israel as God's heir-son.
candidates.source: BibleHub Brenton, Exodus 4; Ancient Faith Ministries, The Sacrifices of Righteousness
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 4 calls Israel God's firstborn, and the Ancient Faith transcript says firstborn in Scripture is a status and the heir.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The firstborn-as-heir teaching is strong, but the source is missing.

questions.question: Complete the warning to Pharaoh: 'If you refuse to let him go, indeed, I will kill your ___ son.'

candidates.text: Already in chapter 4 the final plague is announced: free My firstborn, or your firstborn dies. The threat hangs over all ten plagues and is answered only at Passover, where the blood of the lamb marks the doors and spares the sons within — the lamb of whom no bone was broken, whom Melito proclaims as Christ our Pascha slain for us.
candidates.source: John 19:36; Melito of Sardis, On Pascha
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Chapter 4 flashes the ending early: free My firstborn, or your firstborn dies. Exodus 12 is where that warning lands at Passover.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 4 warns Pharaoh about his firstborn son, and Exodus 12 records the Passover night when Egypt's firstborn died.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Strong hook, but it needs auditable URLs and is too long.

questions.question: Who circumcised her son after the Lord confronted Moses on the journey?

candidates.text: Picture Zipporah's swift flint saving Moses' life. The man sent to demand Pharaoh obey God cannot himself neglect the covenant's sign in his own son; before he can lead the circumcised people to freedom, the seal of the promise must be cut into his own household. Judgment begins at the house of the deliverer.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Roadside shock scene: Zipporah grabs a flint, circumcises her son, and the danger stops.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The image turns into unsourced covenant and judgment commentary.

questions.question: On the journey back to Egypt, the Lord met Moses and sought to kill him until Zipporah circumcised their son.

candidates.text: Picture the terrifying night camp: the Lord moves to kill Moses, and Zipporah seizes a flint, circumcises their boy on the spot, and touches Moses' feet with the blood — blood applied to spare a life, a foreshadow of the Passover doorpost just chapters away. The covenant sign is not optional, even for the deliverer. True.
candidates.styleNotes: current aid

candidates.text: Night camp memory hook: sudden threat, flint, blood, then the attack stops.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The Passover foreshadowing and covenant lesson are unsourced here.
